title: redb use-case and authority contract bounded
supporting_sources: 1.0_VISION/Notebooklm/Architecture Context Matrix_ PostgreSQL-First LifeOS Framework - Table 1.csv

store_authority_alignment.how_this_matrix_settles_the_prior_invent: generated/pgauth_redb_usage_inventory.csv enumerated the same 7 redb record classes but left every classification column at 'pending STORE-001' and carried pre-decision inline commentary asserting that demoting a durable redb use would 'erase a durable use (forbidden by PGAUTH-002 gate)'. STORE-001 is now decided and explicitly corrects that framing (correction_of_prior_brief in store_relationship_decision.json): redb's INTENDED role per the Architecture Blueprint is the transient buffer / local AI scratchpad / application-level WAL / BLAKE3 dedup cache / geometry engine, and PostgreSQL + RuVector is the INTENDED canonical durable owner. This matrix therefore assigns each class its STORE-001-settled classification. It does NOT freeze redb as durable; it honors 'upgrades, never downgrades' through the migration MECHANISM (verified SHA-256 parity cutover), not by preserving a transitional durable role.
store_authority_alignment.no_silent_reclassification_rule: The gate clause 'no existing durable use is silently reclassified' is satisfied precisely: the two currently-durable redb uses (PGREDB-001 CodeDB source_blobs, PGREDB-002 envctl migration_db) are reclassified to durable-TRANSITIONAL with an EXPLICIT, evidence-gated migration path recorded in reclassification_ledger below. Each remains redb-durable until its PostgreSQL replacement passes a green live parity/promotion proof under one preserved SHA-256 identity. The reclassification is documented, gated, and reversible-by-rollback — never assumed, never silent, never lossy.
applied_invariants: Single durable writer: PostgreSQL owns every durable macro-state class exactly once; redb never becomes a second durable writer for a PostgreSQL-owned class.
Forward-only sync: redb -> envctl -> PostgreSQL; no undirected redb<->PostgreSQL dual-write.
Verified cutover: a class leaves redb-durable only after a green live parity/promotion run for that class; never by assumption.
No sidecar: RuVector runs as a native PostgreSQL extension; redb holds no SQL / secondary index / native vector authority (it is a f32 distance-math cache only).
Provenance preserved: every migrated class keeps its SHA-256 identity and its source commit/PR lineage.

gate_contract.verification_gate: Every current and proposed redb record class has a justified use case, owner, lifetime, durability requirement, identity, synchronization edge, crash-replay rule, recovery source, observability signal, and explicit relationship to PostgreSQL and CodeDB; no existing durable use is silently reclassified.
gate_contract.global_clause: no existing durable use is silently reclassified
gate_contract.record_class_universe: The complete, evidence-grounded set of current + proposed redb record classes is PGREDB-001..PGREDB-007. Current (verified in code): PGREDB-001 (CodeDB source_blobs), PGREDB-002 (envctl migration_db). Proposed (blueprint-named mechanisms, not yet adopted): PGREDB-003 scratchpad, PGREDB-004 embedding/dedup cache incl. the f32 geometry-engine compute role, PGREDB-005 application-level WAL/outbox, PGREDB-006 intermediate ingestion buffer, PGREDB-007 shared redb read-view for LifeOS UI. The redb 'high-speed geometry engine' is the f32 distance-math compute function over PGREDB-004 cache vectors, not a distinct record class, and is captured inside PGREDB-004.

record_classes.redb_use: CodeDB source_blobs — content-addressed captured file bytes via codedb_store_redb (redb-backed BlobStore)
record_classes.current_status: current; implemented + parity-tested (blobstore_parity.rs); durable DEFAULT store today
record_classes.justified_use_case: Durable, resumable, content-addressed capture of exact source bytes during CodeDB ingestion; today this is the operating source-of-truth store for captured blobs (codedb_store_redb, 1761 ln, 14 tests).
record_classes.owner: CodeDB capture CLI (single writer per store file)
record_classes.lifetime: per-capture, persists across restarts (long-lived) — TRANSITIONAL under STORE-001
record_classes.durability_requirement: durable TODAY but TRANSITIONAL: STORE-001 sets the target owner to PostgreSQL-canonical; redb is demoted to a transient forward buffer for this class ONLY after the verified BlobStore parity cutover. Until that green run, it remains redb-durable (no data at risk).
record_classes.identity: SHA-256 content hash of file bytes (byte-exact); identical identity across redb and PgStore backends
record_classes.synchronization_edge: forward-only: capture -> store; the peer backend codedb_store_pg::PgStore implements the SAME BlobStore contract, so cutover is a verified swap under one SHA-256 identity, not a dual-write
record_classes.crash_replay_rule: redb single-file ACID: re-open recovers committed state; any missing blobs are re-captured from the working tree (resumable capture skip-set)
record_classes.recovery_source: redb store file + working tree; cutover parity proof crates/codedb_store_pg/tests/blobstore_parity.rs; post-cutover recovery source is PostgreSQL PITR
record_classes.observability_signal: capture receipts, resume skip-set, store schema version (StoreMetadataRow)
record_classes.redb_tier_role: durable-transitional store today -> intended transient forward buffer after cutover
record_classes.relationship_to_postgresql: PostgreSQL + RuVector is the STORE-001 canonical target owner of this durable blob class (state_class 'durable relational macro-state'); redb is subordinate/transitional. Cutover is per-class and verified.
record_classes.relationship_to_codedb: This IS a CodeDB store class — codedb_store_redb is the current backend; codedb_store_pg is its PostgreSQL parity peer under the shared BlobStore trait (ADOPT-001).
record_classes.store_001_class: durable relational macro-state (identities, histories, provenance, code AST/rows, blob-refs)
record_classes.settled_classification: durable-transitional; target PostgreSQL-canonical via verified BlobStore SHA-256 parity cutover

reclassification.cutover_gate: green live crates/codedb_store_pg/tests/blobstore_parity.rs against a disposable PostgreSQL for this class (CDB086 / CDB106-AC05 lane)
reclassification.until_cutover: remains redb-durable; no durable data is lost; SHA-256 identity preserved across backends

evidence_citations: adopted_capabilities.source.csv:BlobStore trait / redb source-blob store / PgStore

record_classes.redb_use: envctl migration_db — replayable migration/orchestration state (targets, runs, run_events, approvals, checkpoints, rollbacks) in redb
record_classes.current_status: current; implemented durable runtime store for envctl (adopted via ADOPT-002)
record_classes.justified_use_case: Durable, replayable migration/orchestration audit and runtime state for the envctl engine's declarative migration plan/apply (migration_db, DbSnapshot).
record_classes.lifetime: durable across the migration lifecycle (long-lived audit history) — TRANSITIONAL for the audit-history portion under STORE-001
record_classes.durability_requirement: durable TODAY but SPLIT under STORE-001: the durable audit HISTORY (settled runs, approvals, rollbacks) is a macro-state class targeting PostgreSQL-canonical via forward promotion; the in-flight runtime orchestration state (uncommitted run working set) may legitimately remain redb-transient. Neither is silently erased.
record_classes.synchronization_edge: forward-only: promote settled run history to PostgreSQL audit tables; envctl redb stays the runtime working store or is cut over per-class — no undirected dual-write
record_classes.crash_replay_rule: redb single-file ACID; envctl's replayable migration engine re-derives state from recorded run_events/checkpoints
record_classes.recovery_source: redb store file + envctl replay engine; post-promotion durable-history recovery source is PostgreSQL PITR
record_classes.redb_tier_role: durable-transitional runtime + audit store today -> transient runtime store after audit-history promotion
record_classes.relationship_to_postgresql: Durable audit history is a PostgreSQL-canonical macro-state class (STORE-001 'histories'); promotion is forward-only and gated. In-flight runtime state stays redb-transient (redb's intended role).
record_classes.relationship_to_codedb: SEPARATE durable redb owner from CodeDB — this is envctl-owned migration state, not CodeDB blobs; the two must be decided independently (explicitly named in the inventory to avoid conflation).
record_classes.store_001_class: durable relational macro-state (histories/provenance) for the audit portion; transient high-frequency state for the in-flight runtime portion
record_classes.settled_classification: durable-transitional (audit history -> PostgreSQL forward promotion); transient (in-flight runtime state -> redb terminal)

reclassification.cutover_gate: green forward-promotion proof of settled envctl run history into PostgreSQL audit tables (envctl crates/engine db_parity.rs lineage), preserving run identity
reclassification.until_cutover: remains redb-durable and replay-recoverable; no audit history is lost

record_classes.redb_use: High-frequency agent scratchpad — transient per-agent thoughts/state changes/terminal traces logged to redb without network lag
record_classes.current_status: proposed; blueprint-named mechanism (Phase 2 Milestone 3 'The Scratchpad'); not a current adopted LifeOS role
record_classes.justified_use_case: Autonomous agents generate a high volume of transient thoughts, terminal traces, and intermediate logs; direct PostgreSQL writes bottleneck the agent loop, so redb absorbs them at process-local speed. This is redb's INTENDED terminal role per the blueprint.
record_classes.owner: owning agent runtime (single writer per scratchpad)
record_classes.durability_requirement: TRANSIENT — no adopted retention/privacy/promotion decision makes this durable; loss on crash is acceptable by definition. This is redb's intended terminal owner class (STORE-001 state_class 'transient high-frequency state').
record_classes.synchronization_edge: forward-only and optional: scratchpad -> envctl flush -> PostgreSQL, where only settled/significant cognitive states are persisted; never authoritative back-write
record_classes.crash_replay_rule: safe to discard on crash (transient by definition); recompute from live agent context
record_classes.recovery_source: none required (disposable); re-created by the agent
record_classes.observability_signal: scratchpad write latency and volume (the microsecond-latency claim RUVPG-CLAIM-018 is UNVERIFIED and must not be asserted as fact)
record_classes.redb_tier_role: transient high-frequency scratchpad (redb intended terminal role)
record_classes.relationship_to_postgresql: Subordinate: only envctl-flushed significant states reach PostgreSQL; PostgreSQL is canonical for anything promoted. redb owns the transient tier here (STORE-001 canonical_owner = redb).
record_classes.relationship_to_codedb: None — unrelated to CodeDB source blobs; different data class.
record_classes.store_001_class: transient high-frequency state (agent thoughts, terminal traces, intermediate logs, state flags)
record_classes.settled_classification: transient-terminal (redb intended owner); no reclassification of any durable use

reclassification.cutover_gate: n/a — redb's intended terminal role; no durable use exists to preserve

record_classes.redb_use: Embedding / dedup cache — model-output cache keyed by BLAKE3 content hash to avoid duplicate inference; includes the redb 'geometry engine' f32 distance-math compute role over cached vectors
record_classes.current_status: proposed; blueprint-named mechanism (envctl dedup cache + geometry engine); no current model-aware cache schema proven
record_classes.justified_use_case: Embedding generation is compute-heavy; redb serves as a semantic cache (BLAKE3 hash -> vector) so envctl never re-embeds an identical code block, and executes f32 distance math (SIMD) over the cached vectors as the 'geometry engine'. A cache miss is always safe.
record_classes.durability_requirement: DISPOSABLE cache — never authority; a miss is always safe (recompute). This is redb's intended cache/geometry role (STORE-001: redb performs f32 distance math only as a cache, with no SQL / secondary index / native vector authority).
record_classes.identity: BLAKE3 hash of (content + model + version + config); collision/invalidation handling is a KNOWN GAP (unproven)
record_classes.synchronization_edge: forward-only: canonical source input -> cache entry; read-only on hit; cache never writes back to authority
record_classes.crash_replay_rule: safe to discard on crash; recompute on miss
record_classes.recovery_source: recompute from source input + local model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2)
record_classes.redb_tier_role: disposable dedup cache + f32 geometry-engine compute (redb intended role)
record_classes.relationship_to_postgresql: Derived from canonical source rows; the durable embeddings/vectors are owned by PostgreSQL + RuVector (native extension, no sidecar). redb never competes as a vector authority (REDB-CLAIM-008/009/010 refuted, 013 qualified).
record_classes.relationship_to_codedb: Derived from CodeDB-captured source content (the hashed input), but holds no CodeDB authority — it is a compute cache over that content.
record_classes.store_001_class: semantic/vector state (derived cache portion) + transient high-frequency state (BLAKE3 dedup hashes)
record_classes.settled_classification: transient-disposable cache (redb intended owner); no reclassification of any durable use

reclassification.cutover_gate: n/a — disposable cache; canonical vectors live in PostgreSQL+RuVector

record_classes.redb_use: Application-level WAL / outbox — redb buffers outgoing PostgreSQL operations during PG latency spikes or outages, retaining until connectivity returns
record_classes.current_status: proposed; blueprint-named mechanism ('redb safely acts as an application-level Write-Ahead Log'); no ordered/idempotent redb->PG outage protocol implemented
record_classes.justified_use_case: When PostgreSQL is busy or offline during intensive GNN operations, the edge node must stay functional; redb buffers PG-destined writes until the connection returns. This is redb's intended resilience role per the blueprint.
record_classes.durability_requirement: DURABLE-UNTIL-ACKED buffer — subordinate to PostgreSQL; must NOT be conflated with PostgreSQL's own internal WAL, nor treated as authority. Capacity/backpressure/ordering/idempotency are KNOWN GAPS (undefined today).
record_classes.identity: monotonic sequence key + operation idempotency key (protocol undefined today — flagged gap)
record_classes.synchronization_edge: forward-only: local op -> outbox -> replay to PostgreSQL with ack; retry + dedup on reconnect; PostgreSQL is the destination authority
record_classes.crash_replay_rule: on crash, replay unacked entries in order; idempotency prevents double-apply (this protocol is NOT yet built — a required build item)
record_classes.recovery_source: redb store file replayed forward into PostgreSQL; PostgreSQL is the recovery authority once acked
record_classes.observability_signal: outbox depth, replay lag, poison-record count
record_classes.redb_tier_role: durable-until-acked application-level WAL/outbox (redb intended resilience role, subordinate to PostgreSQL)
record_classes.relationship_to_postgresql: Strictly subordinate — buffers writes destined for PostgreSQL; PostgreSQL remains canonical; envctl is NOT the synchronizing durable writer (AUTH-CLAIM-001 refuted).
record_classes.relationship_to_codedb: None directly — a generic PG-write outbox, not a CodeDB store; may carry CodeDB-originated ops as payload without owning them.
record_classes.store_001_class: transient high-frequency state (WAL during PostgreSQL latency/outage)
record_classes.settled_classification: transient durable-until-acked buffer subordinate to PostgreSQL (redb intended role); no reclassification of any durable authority

reclassification.cutover_gate: n/a — inherently subordinate/transient; PostgreSQL is always the authority. Build gate: define ordering/idempotency/backpressure protocol before adoption.

record_classes.redb_use: Intermediate ingestion buffer — redb as the transient buffer between parser output and durable PostgreSQL state (NotebookLM 'migration bridge' framing: parser -> redb buffer -> envctl -> PostgreSQL)
record_classes.current_status: proposed TARGET; the pre-decision inventory flagged this as a DIRECT CONTRADICTION with PGREDB-001 (redb durable default) and with verified code (nu_plugin does NOT currently buffer parsed blocks into redb)
record_classes.justified_use_case: The blueprint's Three Pillars of Ingestion define redb as the transient buffer that caches structured AST fragments at low latency before envctl embeds and commits them to PostgreSQL. STORE-001 resolves the contradiction by adopting this buffer-only framing as the TARGET (redb transient), with PGREDB-001's current durable-on-redb default recognized as the transitional artifact that migrates to PostgreSQL-canonical.
record_classes.owner: ingestion pipeline (parser/nu_plugin + envctl bridge) if adopted
record_classes.durability_requirement: TRANSIENT buffer in the STORE-001 target — NOT durable. The current durable behavior belongs to PGREDB-001 and is handled by that class's verified cutover; PGREDB-006 is the intended terminal buffer role once ingestion is wired parser->redb->PG.
record_classes.synchronization_edge: forward-only target: parser -> redb buffer -> envctl (embed) -> PostgreSQL; current reality has no plugin->redb buffering (NUPG-CLAIM-007) — the wiring is the build gap
record_classes.crash_replay_rule: if buffer: replay/re-parse from working tree into redb, then forward to PostgreSQL; unflushed buffer contents are re-derivable from source
record_classes.recovery_source: working tree re-parse (buffer) and, post-ingest, PostgreSQL (durable destination)
record_classes.redb_tier_role: transient ingestion buffer (redb intended terminal role)
record_classes.relationship_to_postgresql: Buffer feeds PostgreSQL, which is the canonical durable destination (STORE-001); redb holds no ingestion authority in the target.
record_classes.relationship_to_codedb: This is the CodeDB ingestion path's buffer tier; it shares SHA-256 identity with PGREDB-001 CodeDB blobs. The contradiction (durable default vs transient buffer) is resolved by PGREDB-001's cutover, not by silently flipping either class.
record_classes.store_001_class: transient high-frequency state (ingestion buffer) feeding durable relational macro-state in PostgreSQL
record_classes.settled_classification: transient buffer (redb intended TARGET role); the durable behavior is owned by PGREDB-001 and migrated via its explicit verified cutover — resolving, not erasing, the durable use

reclassification.cutover_gate: same verified BlobStore parity cutover as PGREDB-001 (the durable default migrates to PostgreSQL; the buffer role is what remains for redb) plus wiring the parser->redb->envctl->PG path
reclassification.until_cutover: current capture stays durable-on-redb (PGREDB-001); the buffer-only target is not asserted as current behavior

record_classes.redb_use: Shared redb read-view — LifeOS UI reads the same redb file agents write, for low-latency swarm-status views over UDS or shared redb state files
record_classes.current_status: proposed; owner-decision-required; not implemented; competes with a UDS-only control path
record_classes.justified_use_case: The blueprint states LifeOS renders the swarm's real-time status by reading the same memory-mapped redb file the agents write, over UDS or shared redb state, to avoid overlapping state and network lag. This is a read-only projection for the UI (interactive_control_path per notebooklm_postgres_context.meta.json).
record_classes.owner: agents write (PGREDB-003 class); LifeOS UI reads (multi-reader, potential shared-file coupling)
record_classes.lifetime: as-durable-as the underlying transient state it mirrors (no independent lifetime)
record_classes.durability_requirement: READ-VIEW — holds NO authority and creates NO second writable owner over agent state. The view is disposable; durability belongs to whatever underlying class it mirrors.
record_classes.identity: shared file path + table identity of the mirrored agent state
record_classes.synchronization_edge: read-only projection for the UI; the interactive control path may instead be UDS with redb-backed views (PGAUTH-004 projection territory); no write edge from the UI
record_classes.crash_replay_rule: UI view is disposable; the underlying agent state recovers per its own family (PGREDB-003)
record_classes.recovery_source: re-read from the canonical agent state; the view holds no authority to recover
record_classes.redb_tier_role: read-only low-latency view over transient agent/redb state (must never become a dual-writer)
record_classes.relationship_to_postgresql: Independent of PostgreSQL durable authority; a low-latency UI view over transient redb state, not a PostgreSQL projection (contrast PGAUTH-004 which projects PostgreSQL to filesystem).
record_classes.relationship_to_codedb: None — different data class (agent swarm status, not CodeDB blobs).
record_classes.store_001_class: derived/interactive view over transient high-frequency state (LifeOS UDS control path)
record_classes.settled_classification: transient read-view (non-authoritative); no reclassification of any durable use; guard against becoming a second writer

reclassification.cutover_gate: n/a — read-view holds no authority; owner decision required on UDS-only vs shared-redb-file access (PGAUTH-004)

reclassification_ledger.purpose: Explicit, non-silent record of every currently-durable redb use and its evidence-gated migration path. Presence of this ledger is what satisfies the gate clause 'no existing durable use is silently reclassified'.

entries.migration_mechanism: codedb_store_pg::PgStore already implements the BlobStore contract; blobstore_parity.rs proves symmetry; per-class verified swap under one preserved SHA-256 identity
entries.cutover_proof_gate: green live crates/codedb_store_pg/tests/blobstore_parity.rs against a disposable PostgreSQL (CDB086 / CDB106-AC05 lane)
entries.loss_risk: none — SHA-256 identity preserved across backends; class stays redb-durable until proof is green

entries.target_owner: PostgreSQL (canonical) for settled audit history; redb-transient for in-flight runtime state
entries.migration_mechanism: forward promotion of settled envctl run history into PostgreSQL audit tables; envctl replayable migration engine re-derives runtime state
entries.cutover_proof_gate: green forward-promotion proof preserving run identity (envctl crates/engine db_parity.rs lineage)
entries.loss_risk: none — replay-recoverable; history preserved through promotion

open_dependencies: Live blobstore_parity.rs green run against a disposable PostgreSQL captures the PGREDB-001 durable-class cutover proof (CDB086 / CDB106-AC05 lane; STORE-001 open dependency; PostgreSQL provisioned this run).

Live RuVector-in-PostgreSQL extension activation not yet locally proven; the semantic/vector aspect of PGREDB-004 caches derives from PostgreSQL+RuVector once activation is green (NBVERIFY-001 / REDB-CLAIM-023 remainder).
PGREDB-005 outbox ordering/idempotency/backpressure protocol is undefined and must be designed before adoption.
PGREDB-007 UDS-only vs shared-redb-file access is an owner decision deferred to PGAUTH-004.
hold: Final activation/migration of any class is held for the owner discussion represented by STORE-001 (decided) and its gated cutover proofs; this matrix is a planning classification, not a store mutation.
